comments.title: Opening/Closing Prayer Gestures and Gnosticism and Hermeticism
comments.content:
Good Evening,

May September bring some new good things to you and yours.

To Share: After a few months of experimenting, I've hit on a different opening and closing prayer/self-blessing/physical gesture. Most directly useful to Heathens, but possibly helpful to others: https://jpowellrussell.com/#opening_and_closing_prayer_with_gestures

To Ask: In "The Dialectical Faith of Leftism," James Lindsay argued that Hegel smashed together "Gnosticism" and "Hermeticism." When it came to "Gnosticism," it was the typical "world is an iron prison, the demiurge and his agents are oppressive, and the secret knowledge needed to escape this is available to sophisticated adepts" flavor of gnosticism that gets trotted out as "the" gnostic belief often enough these days, so that wasn't a huge surprise. More interestingly, perhaps, Lindsay claims that Hegel's whole theory of the dialectic, and the idea that reality can be "perfected" through processes that identify and bring out the "divine spark" within every piece of experienced reality, is a variant of hermetic alchemy. His description showed enough familiarity with hermeticism to be interesting, at least, but there were definitely a few points where his polemical intent and/or disdain for the underlying metaphysics came through. Interesting piece, in that I frequently found myself thinking "hey, not so fast, you're not being fair there," while still mostly agreeing with the conclusions. Anyhow, my actual question is: do we have any evidence as to whether Hegel really was familiar with any flavor of hermetic belief, or even more so, adopted it as a core influence on his philosophy?
- (For anyone curious, here's the extra-short version of the talk's main conclusion: Rousseau invented the idea of the "social contract," which said "to get as much freedom as possible, we have to give up some freedom" and also put a lot of emphasis on how important society's views on reality are, rather than reality itself. Hegel, partially inspired by Rousseau, took Kant's Thesis, Antithesis, and Synthesis and applied to an idealist system inspired by Heremeticism, where there's true Being, which manifests imperfectly in Becoming in the form of "sparks", but in manifesting, these sparks lose their sense of their own divinity. By exposure to other manifest things with their own divine sparks, they work out what it means to act better. Through this dialectic process, reality would eventually be perfected to true Being. Marx took this view and made it strictly materialist, and then later thinkers applied the thinking to culture, or identity groups, or whatever.)

As always, thanks very much to JMG and everyone else here for all that you do.

My blessings and best wishes to all who welcome them,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Opening/Closing Prayer Gestures and Gnosticism and Hermeticism
replies.content: There's a tolerably well-known book published in 2001, Hegel and the Hermetic Tradition by Glenn Magee, which argues that Hegel was influenced by the Hermetic tradition. I haven't read it, as Hegel isn't at all to my taste; I know that the book is still considered controversial and a good many scholars disagree with it. The attempt to equate Hegelianism with Gnosticism strikes me as very dubious, partly because not that much was known about Gnosticism in Hegel's time, and partly because claiming that the world can be perfected is antithetical to the kind of Gnosticism Lindsay is talking about. But I haven't read Lindsay's arguments either, of course.

replies.title: Re: Opening/Closing Prayer Gestures and Gnosticism and Hermeticism
replies.content:
Magee's book is interesting -- but as the title indicates the focus is on "the Hermetic tradition" and not Gnosticism as such (whatever the "such" may happen to be for any particular writer!).

Magee is interested, among other things, in the relationship between Hegel and Boehme, and thus with the current of German philosophizing that stems from Boehme (as well as the currents that led up to him). He doesn't suppose that Hegel was knowledgeable about Gnosticism, but of course there's Lurianic Kabbalah, and according to Scholem, Kabbalah is Jewish Gnosticism, and there you are. Gnosticism is like spinach for some people, who can detect it no matter how much it is disguised or diluted ("I say it's spinach and I say the hell with it").

This is what happens when people know a lot by reading about things, without actually knowing things directly, or in this case reading and digesting the texts they think they know about because they've heard what other people have to say about what others have said about them.

Much of this goes back to Eric Voegelin's nuanced and reflective comparison of certain Gnostic currents with certain modes of political thought, which he modified over the course of his career as he learned and thought more. Voegelin's account of Gnosticism has become the Dulcinea of some modern writers, who are deeply in love with it although they have never actually met it.

replies.title: Re: Opening/Closing Prayer Gestures and Gnosticism and Hermeticism
replies.content: Ha! "The Dulcinea of some modern writers" is a keeper. Thank you.

Something troubling has occurred to me after reading the post on karma, and I'd like to know if you think it plausible. Back around 2005 or so, a somewhat interesting idea caught on in some of the local Neopagan scene: namely, that there was a way to curse people without cursing them, and in fact, arguably do them a favor. The idea was that if you hit people with a spell to force all of their karma on them at once, it would hurt them, but in a way which would be good for them in the long term. There was plenty of talk about how it would even help them change for the better, so it was arguably doing them a favor, blah, blah, blah.

The troubling possibility which has occurred to me is that since you discussed karma as working in large part by character, these workings might have accelerate the descent into evil character. By the raspberry jam principle, these kinds of workings are likely to also accelerate the descent of the people casting them into negativity. Given that anything like calling all of someone's karma on them and insisting its for their benefit is sleazy, and a justification for evil, the moment they did those workings they'd establish momentum to become sleazy and prone to justifying evil, at the same time they called in energies to accelerate such changes in themselves.

replies.content:
While "calling all of someone's karma on them and insisting its for their benefit" is certainly an act of dubious merit, it is no more "a justification for evil" than it is a justification for good. What it is is a justification for rapid karma or the expediting of consequences. Those with a surplus of good karma would experience blessing from such a working, while those with excessive bad karma would experience cursing. If those workings turned out to be at all effective (which I rather doubt,) they would indeed "have accelerate[d] the descent into evil character" of some, but, at the same time, they also would have accelerated the ascent into benevolent character of others.

Mind you, I am in no way endorsing that particular Neopagan hypocrisy, as I'm quite sure that very few of its casters had any intention of letting karma decide the actual outcome of their working. Most would have approached it with an eager intention to curse, with no desire to do any favor, and with a pathetically weak legalistic argument vainly smeared over their ill-will in an attempt to protect themselves from its inevitable blowback.

All I'm arguing is that with goodwill and an intention to bless, simply asking karma to be expedited is neither evil nor sleazy. It is presumptuous and attempting to usurp the divine, or at least stress out the divine by unduly rushing it. Karma is the natural way of the universe. Figuring out how to align one's will with karma's divine will would be an extraordinarily balancing practice. Rushing karma, on the other hand, would probably stress out the caster to no end, while having barely any impact on karma itself. Pretending that we have control over the forces of the universe, gods or otherwise, really tends to annoy them, which then always generates a lot of unwanted blowback.

Poor Neopagans, imagining that lying about their true intentions could somehow hoodwink the gods and help them to escape from karma’s reach! From the spectacular implosion clobbering the Neopagan scene over the past decade, I’d say the only karma they really managed to get delivery speeded up on was their own. “What, you didn’t order raspberry jam? Oh, that’s such a shame, because that is what you got!”

— Christophe

replies.title: Re: Two Questions

1) Not unless you're a Pokémon! (Hint: you're not.)

2) Of course. Your higher self, your soul, and the divine spark that guides you are all immortal. What are those? Your higher self is who you really are, the individuality behind all the personalities you take on in different incarnations. The divine spark is also known as your guardian angel; it's an emanation from the divine that helps to guide and protect you on your journey of spiritual evolution. All three of those are immortal.

replies.title: Re: Two Questions
replies.content:
This is likely to be quite different than the wisdom that JMG offers, but I'd like to comment on this question from the perspective of the Heathen soul lore that I practice and write about.

In my perspective we have multiple souls, eight of them, each with distinctive footholds in our physical life and life in Midgard. (Ferah, Ahma, Ghost, Hama, Aldr, Mod, Hugr, Saiwalo.) Some souls are more focused on Midgard, others on our present, past and future existences in other Worlds, but they're all gathered together into our 'soular system' or soul-household. While we are in this life in Midgard, our 'soular system' resonates or sings together, more or less harmoniously and effectively depending on many factors.

The song that is thus sung into being by our souls is our 'sense of self' in this life, in this world. This is our ninth soul, our Sefa, the ninth wave of our being in this life, and it is focused on everything that is implied by 'relationship': the relationships among all our souls, our relationships with other people, the natural world, spiritual beings, the relationships among intangibles like ideas, theories, images--everything where meaning and experience are enhanced by tangible and intangible relationships.

During this life Sefa operates as something like a hologram, a complex image formed from, and storing, the life experiences and perceptions of all our souls. After we die our souls separate in different directions; each has its own afterlife trajectory. When a hologram is broken into parts, each part still retains the entire image, though some of the details may be blurry. In the same way, our Sefa distributes its holographic self among all the other souls at death. The souls who survive physical death--not all of them do--continue on their afterlife trajectories bearing this hologram of their life together, with details somewhat blurred, but still a sense of who they were during life is retained.

In this perspective 'selfhood' as we usually understand it is tied to this-life, our present life in Midgard. But each of our souls has its own selfhood--this is why they are multiple, not single: they are each a 'self' with all that implies. During life, after and between lives, the individual souls go through the kinds of spiritual development that we learn of in occult studies, each in their own way. So the larger, beyond-one-lifetime question of 'what / who is my self?' leads us off in different directions depending on which soul we're talking about, or speaking from.

Well, manybe this has some relevance to your question, maybe not! Good luck in your search, wherever your gaze may fall!

Winifred Hodge Rose

Take another look at the grammar. "Me" is what things belong to; "my" means "in relation to me." So saying "my me" is like wondering whether your hands have hands, or whether a coffee cup is that coffee cup's coffee cup.

"My higher self" is simply a roundabout way of saying "the real me" -- it's relevant because people confuse their personality with their real self, the higher self.

Parenting can be a very stressful role, especially if one goes into it with lots of unresolved fears weighing them down. Was either of your parents overly afraid of wilderness areas or of all the bumps, bruises, and injuries one can get from engaging in physical labor? Did either of them refrain from expressing any overt fears about outdoor activities, but only ever express any real enthusiasm about indoor activities and man-made environments?

If someone other than your parents had an unusual level of influence over you at some point, such as a sibling, a teacher, or a lover, they also could have cast nature-avoiding spells over you. Those would be somewhat less likely to have been invoked benevolently or protectively. Siblings sometimes jealously guard whatever domain they have managed to excel in, so a sporty, yard-working brother could curse his siblings in order to protect his accomplishments from any rivals. Lovers sometimes try to stifle any conflict or contest by controlling or changing their partner, so a nature-phobic spouse could curse their nature-loving partner in order to "save the relationship."

If you think you can identify someone who was likely to have cast these kind of spells over you, doing the Order of Spiritual Alchemy lessons of the Octagon Society would be a great way to unearth what was done and to begin the work of dispelling it. Every aspect of your human organism, including its exquisitely sensitive nervous system, evolved while living and working in nature. Spending time outside in nature tends to rebalance us back to more primordial concerns and away from man-made stresses. If that is instead leaving you feeling drained and depressed, something is messing around with your nervous system’s innate tendencies, and I doubt that whatever that is is your friend.

— Christophe

Hi! I've been dealing with active Kundalini triggered by ceremonial magic practice for five years. I have gotten a lot of mileage out of remedial deep tissue massages, Epsom salts baths and long stretches to reduce the overall level of nervous system activity. Supplementing with magnesium has also been vital. Cold shower can calm the energy activity but are also great for knocking you out of depressed or anxious states of mind.

The book 'Biology of Kundalini' by Jana Dixon (can be read online for free) has extensive recommendations about supplements, practices and other aspects to support people with Kundalini activation.

As it sounds like you are experiencing, intense Kundalini activations act as something like a chronic health condition and complex mental illness rolled into one. Activity levels may vary over the years but if you are actively spiritual, it will likely keep bubbling away consistently for years. I am led to believe that you do come out the other side, but I am yet to get there, despite Kundalini emerging from my crown two and a half years ago.

Overall, I would say strap yourself in for an adventure that might last years, work out how best to support your nervous system and be kind to yourself by allowing your expectations about your participation in everyday life to come to reflect your capabilities rather than redundant pre-awakening expectations.

When you say incapacitated, I assume you aren't working. If that's sustainable for you, that's very fortunate. It may be possible to work down the track. I worked full time for years after my awakening. I was very burnt out most of the time, but I learnt to function by putting the intense thought patterns, emotions and sometimes even visions to one side. I regret pushing myself that hard and don't recommend it at all.

Best of luck working through it!

On 2), regarding psychotherapy:

I started going to therapy after things got a little rough after my mom died. I started the therapy before I got into JMG's spiritual practices, and have continued it afterwards, and have talked with my therapist about it to some degree. I was deeply worried/embarrassed to bring it up, as I had largely internalized the idea that non-standard religion was in some sense "silly" or "not serious," and even more so the PMC cultural trends that see religion of any sort as somehow "dumb" or unsophisticated. Once I actually explained what my experience had been and what it had meant to me, though, my therapist said something like "I wish more people would explain spiritual experience the way you did," and I found that an unexpected boost to my confidence in thinking about and talking about the topic.

As for the method of therapy, she doesn't seem to follow any particular "school," though like most active professionals these days, much of what she does borrows from Cognitive-Behavioral Therapy (CBT), which is the most common methodology adopted by therapists. CBT seems to be pretty compatible with the kind of intellectual, reflective spiritual practices JMG teaches - it's basically secularized, psychologized Stoicism, so that's not too surprising. I've brought up Jungian and other more symbolic ways of understanding things, but she doesn't seem to be too familiar with that outside of what I bring up. For the most part, therapists ought to be letting you do most of the talking anyway, so the framework they bring to understanding what you're saying will likely have far less to do with how the therapy goes than whether you have a good rapport, they're open to you talking about your woo-woo experiences, and so forth.

In my own case, possibly a relevant point that happened by accident: my therapist was raised Hindu, and I get the impression from some of her comments that she's at least indifferently religious. There's some chance that this background has made her more open to polytheistic spiritual experiences than someone raised monotheist or secular might be.

Anyhow, hope this helps!
Jeff

comments.title: Discursive meditiation and planes of being
comments.content:
Greetings, JMG and all!

This is more of a comment than a question, following on your very useful guidelines for the technique of discursive meditation. (In a nutshell, this involves selecting a particular seed for thought and following threads of its implications to achieve a deeper understanding of it. As our mind strays during this process, we are to follow the thread of the straying thought back to the original theme, thus learning more about our own thought processes and our own nature.)

This is fine training for developing the mental body. I suggest that the same technique is equally useful for the emotional / astral domains of our existence. Taking a particular emotion or emotionally tinged thought, image, or trait, and following its implication-threads in a focused way clarifies our emotional life and contributes to the growth of good character and disciplined use of our emotional powers.

Lately I've been experimenting to see how well discursive meditation works at the spiritual level, a level where often the 'contemplation' method of meditation is used, something more still and receptive, less active, than discursive meditation. It's an interesting challenge!

From where do we get our seeds and threads for discursive meditation? From thoughts--the wise thoughts of others expressed in books and sayings, or our own thoughts and experience. From emotions, traits of character, situations in our life that we want to clarify and perhaps to change.

What about at the spiritual level--where would the seeds for discursive meditation come from? What I find is that they come from contemplative meditation: from contact with Deities, other spiritual beings at that level, or simply with the natural energies of that plane of existence.

Here's the part that needs some practice, because if we're used to being handed a ready-made seed or idea for discursive meditation, such as from your writing or something we've drawn from our own journal, then the discursively-trained mind immediately begins seeking around for one at the spiritual level. We may not find anything right away because at this level we are not dealing with the thoughts or emotions we've been trained to pursue discursively.

If we shift into contemplative mode, absorbing spiritual influences from a Deity or stream of spiritual power, then eventually when that contemplation has ripened as it needs to, a seed or a thread will be held out to us from that source.

But it's tricky, because that seed is not a thought nor an emotion. It is a thread of Being, in my experience from a Deity--a thread or seed of that Deity's own being--which we are invited to follow as we would follow the implication-threads of a wise thought or an emotion in need of clarification. But at this level, it needs to be done without our usual thought or emotional processes; if we do use them we fall out of that plane into a different one (which is also good--merging our experiences of different planes can be enlightening, but it throws us out of spiritual-discursive meditation).

So we need to stay attuned to the Deity / flow of spiritual power in contemplative mode, while also pursuing the spiritual thread or seed we've been handed in discursive mode, but in a different way than we're used to....in my experience, this feels like one of those childhood challenges to pat your head, rub your belly, and skip in a circle all at the same time! But it does feel like it has interesting potential...

So anyway, thanks for your patience if you've made it this far, and thanks for your teaching on discursive meditation!

Winifred Hodge Rose

replies.title: Re: Discursive meditiation and planes of being
replies.content: Thank you for this. Yes, the method of discursive meditation has some remarkable potentials; people knew all about those in past centuries, but we're still rediscovering them now.

replies.title: Re: Discursive meditiation and planes of being
replies.content:
My, my, my Winifred, your comment totally blew my mind! Thank you SO much for this.
And, HA! I actually wrote this out before JMG commented because I didn’t want to jump in prematurely but I guess this actually re-enforces his comment so here we go!

I am working on memorizing the first portion of Patanjali’s Yoga Sutras in Sanskrit. Though I’m not a Sanskrit scholar, I still find much benefit in “singing”/chanting in that language – it gives me inspiration and insight even though I don’t always know the English translation of all the individual words. And the Sutras are very cryptic to begin with even for a scholar, so… That leaves me with
1. Contemplation, in a “wordless way” while chanting hoping to open up to inspiration and insight
2. Looking up various translations in English for the “word and language” help

I currently am up to the last few verses in the first Chapter where Patanjali lists (he’s very big on lists;-) the various levels of Samadhi (Samadhi: the Yogic state of mind where one abides in one’s true nature of Soul/Higher Self/Divine Nature.)
Patanjali let’s us know (in the second Chapter on Practice 2.1) that Yoga practice towards the Yogic state is three-fold:
1. Tapas – management of the nature forces (emotional and character development)
2. Svadhyaya – study and mental development
3. Ishvara Pranidhana – devotion to “the Lord” (of choice)
(Coincidentally, this is the Three-Fold Way towards Magic of our dear D.F!)

Patanjali’s levels of Samadhi have two broad categories: SA-BIJA “with seed”, and NIR-BIJA “without seed”. Your use of words and coherent thoughts here just really opened my mind towards these concepts that I was having a challenging time with! I look forward to re-reading your comment for further insights!
Allow me to list these last few Sutras as translated by Barbara Stoler Miller, if you might care to “hear” them. And thank you again.
CONTEMPLATION THAT BEARS SEEDS
1.42 When concepts formed from knowledge based on words and their meanings taint it, contemplative poise is broken by conjecture
1.43 When memory is purified, then contemplative poise is free of conjecture, empty of its own identity, with the object shining forth
1.44 Contemplative poise that is both reflective and intuitive, with elements as its objects is explained by this
1.45 The subtlety of objects results in their being free of defining marks
1.46 These modes of contemplative poise are the contemplation that bears seed

TOWARDS SEEDLESS CONTEMPLATION
1.47 The profound clarity of intuitive cognition brings inner tranquility
1.48 Here wisdom is the vehicle of truth
1.49 It has a different scope than scriptural or inferential knowledge because its object is singular
1.50 A subliminal impression generated by wisdom stops the formation of other impressions
1.51 When the turnings of thought cease completely, even wisdom ceases, and contemplation bears no seeds


Thank again All!

replies.title: Re: Discursive meditiation and planes of being
replies.content:
The thing is, Jeff, that I find 'practice' more effective than 'sources,' though I know that can sound frustrating. Here are some examples. I really got into the spiritual observances of Advent, Lent, and especially the Holy Week observances before and during Easter. I sang in the choir, and this was a gruelling commitment of time, effort, and devotion, which paid off equally richly, especially during these times of special celebration. The choir, and music generally, was a major element of all the observances.

I practiced the Stations of the Cross during holy week, where our church set up meditation stations around the sanctuary to meditate on the steps Christ took toward his crucifixion. I participated in Evensong and in nightly vigils in the beautifully decorated little side-chapel. I fasted in various ways. I loved the music, the art and architecture, the little traditional details and customs. I used the Book of Common Prayer and the book of Lesser Feasts and Fasts, both of which contain very beautiful prayers. I sang plainsong devotions at home and observed the canonical hours. This wasn't studying something so much as it was living it.

It was honestly very hard for me to leave all this, and leave behind good friends with whom I shared it. Sometimes I felt I was being kidnapped by the Heathen Gods! But the leaving was a true movement of my souls, a necessity for me. And most of my observances now have moved from the physical plane types of practices I described, to metaphysical planes.

But as far as a 'method' for this, truly I think the best method for learning contemplation is through inspiration rather than instruction, and what inspires me might not inspire you. Well-known writers on contemplation and mysticism include Thomas Merton and Evelyn Underhill, and I've read most of their work, but it didn't really advance me that much though it was good reading. I liked St. Julian of Norwich better, St Teresa of Avila, and the poetry of St. John of the Cross. St. Ignatius of Loyola has good exercises. I think some of these are in JMG's reading lists.

I'd like to offer an article of my own here: "Earth, Water, Wind and Fire: Elemental Modes for Relating to the Deities". I use the elements as a shorthand or analogy for different ways of experiencing and relating to the Deities; some of these ways would certainly qualify as 'contemplative.'

https://heathensoullore.net/earth-water-wind-and-fire-elemental-modes-for-relating-to-the-deities/

But again, I don't offer a precise 'method' here, so much as a description of the mode, some examples from my experience, a few considerations and precautions, and perhaps some inspiration to try it out. Like a child learning how to engage and deal with and interact with the world around her, we learn this art by doing, along with plenty of trial and error, and serendipitous discoveries along the way.

Winifred

replies.title: Re: Discursive meditiation and planes of being
replies.content:
Dear Winifred, thanks a lot for this post! May I ask you a question? Do you know of a good set of instructions, similar in simplicity and clarity to JMG's instructions for discursive meditation, that are for contemplative meditation?

Thanks in advance, and no worries if nothing comes to mind!

replies.title: Re: Discursive meditiation and planes of being
replies.content:
As far as instructions for contemplation, there are a great many approaches out there, and I wouldn't presume to say that one way, or one religion's way, is better than another. It also makes a difference whether one chooses contemplation as a devotional practice directed toward one's Deities, versus contemplation as a more detached, open-ended spiritual practice.

I can mention a few things based on my own preference, which is to learn more from example than from methodology. I find that 'instruction' is counterproductive for contemplation, for the very reason that we get caught up at the mental level when we try to understand and follow instructions. The very effort of dealing with words themselves can be counterproductive. As long as we're engaged on the mental level, this acts as kind of a drag-anchor on our efforts to function at the spiritual level.

The koans of Zen Buddhism offer an example of what I'm saying from the standpoint of detached spiritual practice--just reading and pursuing the koans, without any 'instructions' about how to do it. That was one of my forms of training, many years ago--reading the koans in books; I didn't have access to classes. Koans use words, yes, but they are so deliberately illogical that they can tie our mental body up in knots, and while it's sorting itself out, we can zip off and get a taste of what the spiritual plane is like! :-)

One of my very favorite koans is "what is the value of a dead cat?" And the master's response is: "it is priceless, because you can't put a price on it." I enjoy the fits that my mind throws when faced with this ridiculous statement; the humorous aspect of it is so freeing to the spirit! And there are the mind-expanding koans...my 3-year old daughter came up with a good one (50 years ago!) when I was singing her to sleep one night, and she asked me "where does a song go after you sing it?" I was awake most of the night after that, roaming among the stars I guess, chasing down that song... She also got very upset with me when I couldn't explain to her where an egg goes after it is scrambled--I think she was referring to its Platonic Ideal of egg-hood. That's one that I have not solved, to this day!

But my preferred practice is devotional, and there I've learned most from reading about, and in a few cases knowing, people who practiced contemplative devotion enough that it shaped their whole approach to life.

One of the books that made an impression on me while still Christian was "The Practice of the Presence of God" by Brother Lawrence, a lay brother of the Carmelite Order living in the 1600s in Paris. It's a very short and simple book, written by a simple, uneducated man who didn't feel he had anything important to say but wrote it in obedience to his superior. It's definitely Christian, and non-Christians as I am now need to do a bit of filtering, but I found it heart-felt and inspiring in its very simplicity. It bypasses the mind's grip, due to its simplicity and non-intellectuality, and engages the emotions only in the sense that it is appealing, but not emotionally gripping. Sort of boring, really, but in a nice way! What it does is, by force of example, awaken the devotional attention and power of the heart.

For me, this is what devotional contemplation is: a movement of the heart outward toward the heart's true desire, and an acceptance of whatever response is given back to that gesture of our spiritual heart. Even when there is no response; even when the response is incomprehensible; even when it is overwhelming and drives us to change our whole life.

It's so simple, really, though not at all easy: open your heart toward your spiritual desire. Actually focus on your physical heart, feel its energy expand outward, feel its warmth, the very core of your life. As with other forms of meditation, when distractions arise, simply gently return to your outflowing heart. Our heart knows where to go, what to reach for, even if we don't; trust it and don't argue with it when it's focused on the spiritual realms. Deep in our hearts lies a Mystery of Being that doesn't begin to grow into our awareness until we reach into these spiritual realms. We awaken that mystery by our own practice; there's no other way to do it.

Sorry...I am writing entirely too much here today! Blessings to all who wish for them!

Winifred

comments.title: Keeping an eye on what happened to them personally
comments.content:
I was very intrigued by this statement of yours, “Whether someone comes across as an insincere actor is as irrelevant to my point of view as any other detail of their personality; the question in my mind is whether their teachings work in practice -- though I do tend to keep an eye on what happened to them personally, since that's one guide to how well their system works in the long run.”

1. How do you discover what happened to them personally? Using the web, bookstores and libraries, I tend to find very little and most of that is promotion, e.g., books written by people promoting their own school or version of whatever occult thing they “invented.” I read Martin Booth’s biography of Aleister Crowley, which seems like a good reason to avoid Thelemite magic.

I was intrigued by sigil magic as used by Chaos magicians because it seems to be sound psychologically, yet Austin Osman Spare, who is credited with developing the type of sigil magic used by them is said to have lived and died in poverty and obscurity. I’m not implying that fame and fortune are measures of success, but I have not been able to find out much about him other than the poverty and obscurity.

I was intrigued by Chaos Magic because it seems like a call to wildcraft your magic, the same way that Druidry requires that you wildcraft your Druidry. Yet Druidry seems to call for wildcrafting upon a base of certain values.

2. Do you think that it is a lack of a values base that causes Chaos magicians to end up calling up demons and negative archtypes?

replies.title: Re: Keeping an eye on what happened to them personally
replies.content:
1) This is one of the reasons I mostly study books by dead people; it's much easier to figure out how things turned out in the long run, especially if biographies have been written about them. (There are several good bios of Spare, btw.)

2) Most Chaos magicians believe that gods and demons don't actually exist -- they're simply magically charged images ("hypersigils") created by the human imagination. That leaves them hopelessly vulnerable to manipulation by actual spiritual beings, including evil ones. Combine that with the tendency of people in avant-garde culture these days to get into the faux-edginess of "Look at me, I'm evil!" -- as though that hasn't been a tired cliché since the 1880s! -- and you have the fad for demonolatry in the Chaos magic scene.

replies.title: Re: Keeping an eye on what happened to them personally
replies.content: Could you recommend some bios of Spare? I would love to read one or two.

replies.title: Re: Keeping an eye on what happened to them personally
replies.content: The one I'd recommend is Phil Baker's bio Austin Osman Spare: The Occult Life of London's Legendary Artist. It's very solid and well-researched.

replies.title: Re: Comet Nishimura
replies.content:
I computed them from ecliptic coordinates. I took the Right Ascension and Declination coordinates, and fed them through one of the various online converters to convert these to ecliptic coordinates.

Since 0 degrees longitude ecliptic equals 0 degrees Aries, 30 degrees longitude equals 0 degrees Taurus, 60 degrees longitude equals 0 degrees Gemini, and so on. To double check my work, I cross-referenced with an ecliptic map as offered by Sky and Telescope ( https://shopatsky.com/products/ecliptic-map ).

For the discovery data, the RA and Dec coordinated are listed in Astronomical Telegram that marked its discovery: http://www.cbat.eps.harvard.edu/iau/cbet/005200/CBET005285.txt

The magnitude data is taken from https://www.cobs.si/

As far as current coordinates, I used https://theskylive.com/c2023p1-info for live data.

replies.title: Re: GSF SOP

replies.title: (no subject)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Carl Spiesberger in Magische Praxis p.88 writes (my rough translation) of the Ankh cross or Crux ansata or Nilschlüssel (Nile key) or handle/life cross:
"The person of five senses (Tau) has succeeded in producing the connection with the higher senses (circle: spirit over matter). The boundary is broken through. The mortal ego unites with its eternal core, or as the esoteric expresses: the key to the eternal self is found. Considered thus the life cross symbolises at the same time the seven principles or basic parts of human nature. The Tau [Greek T] stands for the quaternary vanishing into death, the circle for the imperishable, the higher triad. The handle cross four and finds use in many variations as a protective sign."

That was the first of 5 paragraphs on it, but I think I'm running out of time to post this.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: In the rest of Spiesberger's discussion of it (the book has a section of30 pages discussing various occult symbols and their meanings), he relates it to beauty in architecture, ie the softening that rounded shapes bring to the hard edges of triangles and rectangles, "The rounded columns of the Greeks on firmly based foundations - the world of facts bound with the ideal, the beautiful. […] The human as representative of two expanses of being - rooting in the material, fulfilling his duty on earth, striving for his true home."

In fact, people were sometimes uncomfortable that I found anyone attractive and wanted anything other than to focus only on math and perhaps stock trading and donating some earnings to charity. I've been on a couple dates that went nowhere - after a decade of trying - and am wondering what I might do.

I've become deeply "blackpilled" and have come to believe that because I am short, ugly, balding, and on the spectrum to boot I'll never have a partner that's better than being alone. I've heard some suggestions...going abroad for a partner, volunteering at soup kitchens in hopes of meeting a desperate woman that would care more about money than my physical appearance or social grace, or simply accepting that I'll never be in a relationship. I've also heard that there was some kind of wisdom or social skill that you could ONLY get as a result of being in a relationship...which is why I'm OK with things like my partner being addicted to heroin or being crazy enough to try and stab me or smash up my car or whatever. Most people in these kinds of situations survive, and I'll be more socially graceful and wiser at the end of it.

I also wonder if there's something karma-related; regretfully, I've been tempted to start volunteering in soup kitchens in order to find women who might be OK with an ugly balding autist. Or join 12-step groups and look for a heroin addict or alcoholic. Things like that. To me, they seem sleazy - but are there honorable ways to have sex and relationships with women like this? I know I'm no catch...but people have said that it's pretty gross to go for women that can't work jobs or keep a roof over their head on a consistent basis, if you're someone who's got a stable job and a place to live.

Is there any way to become more socially graceful, or at least more honorable/less sleazy, and thus get better character? Or: is there some kind of Left Hand Path or consequentialist stuff, such that I'd only be able to do certain good and honorable things AFTER having done gross, messed-up things and getting the wisdom for that? Like...you have to have some practice in order to get good, and maybe dealing with different kinds of tragedy and misery gives you the wisdom to have a relationship with someone that can manage to be a good enough mother to not kill or maim the kids.

What's your recommendation here? I'm honestly interested in hearing this...is the path to improve my character, or to hop into the muck that is dating as a deeply unattractive and disgusting man?

replies.title: Re: Sleaziness and finding a partner
replies.content:
There are plenty of other options. I'm also well over on the autism spectrum and not anybody's idea of Adonis, and I've been married for 39 years. The crucial steps are, first, deal with your emotional baggage -- the Order of Spiritual Alchemy work is good for that:

https://octagonsociety.org/preliminary-lessons/

And second, figure out how you're sabotaging yourself, and stop. Daily meditation and divination can help quite a bit with that. The soup kitchen/drug addict business is another guarantee of failure, btw -- I know people who went that route and the result was a smoking crater. Oh, and consider joining the Masons or some other such group -- that's one good way to learn social skills. (Again, it worked for me.)

comments.title: MAD
comments.content:
A question.

When I was ten, I engaged in self-harming behavior; had tantrums, had a lot of behavior problems. Partly a result of undiagnosed autism. Parents tried to scare feces clean out of me by threatening to send me to a psych ward one particularly terrible day. I was told about stuff like straightjackets and padded rooms and being hosed down with ice water for misbehaving and other messed-up bovine-derived fertilizer. Including potentially being taken advantage of by the psych-unit version of Bubba. Said that the local ward wasn't going to take kids for another three months, but once it did, all bets were off if I wound up pulling anything like that again. It in fact scared the feces clean out of me. I proceeded to call a family meeting after six days; my terms were:

- That I was not to use violence against my parents.

- That my younger sibling, then six, was not to be involved in our conflict.

- That if something [such as being preyed upon by Bubba] was to happen - if my younger sibling wound up in a psych unit and something terrible happened - that all bets were then off and that I would seek justice by whatever means necessary. At the time I didn't mind dying in order to avenge such a terrible thing, and was extremely clear about that.

Three days after that, after a bunch of sleepless nights listening to my parents talk at night and whatnot, I succeeeded. I'd been inspired by one of my history textbooks. Nikita Kruschev made plausibly deniable threats of nuclear anihilation in order to prosecute the Cold War with the US; I did the same with self-deletion in order to buy myself more time to correct my behavior. At the time, it felt like I was invoking powerful and slightly malevolent forces to protect me - and I had the distinct sense that I would be protected, but for a price. It was one that I was willing to pay, and even now fifteen years later do not regret much.

If this was some kind of magic, what effect did it have? Of note, my sibling's doing fine. Some mental health concerns as an early teenager, but now doing very well.

I'm the "Sleaze and finding a partner" guy, for what it's worth. Hell of a Faustian bargain, if indeed it was. Eh. Sib's doing fine.

replies.title: Re: MAD
replies.content: That's not magic, it's simply a harsh but necessary act of will. You did what you needed to do. I'm going to reiterate my suggestion of the OSA work, though; your parents should have been flogged, and I suspect you're dealing with a lot of emotional damage from the abuse they subjected you to.

Assuming that I survive, I feel that this ordeal is going to freeze the disgusting unattractiveness off of my character. There is a chance that my physical body will freeze along with it, which I believe is part of the value - indeed, most of the value - of this whole concept. Being tried as gold is tried in the fire. Slogging through 30 below zero temperatures on skis in the Alaskan wilderness. I believe that if I survive this - and my intention is to go on Feb. 26 2024 - that I'll gain a great deal from this and have Chadly perseverance and toughness. Is there anything that I can do to make this work, or be more effective? The wilderness provides through victory, or death...at least, I sure hope so.

comments.content:
A while back, I discussed the possibility of changes with planets marking changes in the laws of nature. It seems to be the case that the laws of nature, or at least the perception of them, change with new planets. I've so far looked at four planets: Uranus, Ceres, Vulcan, and Pluto. I plan to add Neptune and Eris to the list as well, but I haven't started yet.

Uranus seems to have ties to electricity and explosives. There are various changes in how plenty of things are understood; and in some cases, to provide one example, it's hard to reconcile some of the old alchemical writings with modern chemistry; simply put, there are various procedures described in medieval and older alchemy which, were people to do them today, would result in explosions. I've seen some of them discussed in sources from the early modern era where people are flagging some of the traps, and several things which would produce explosives are not flagged.

Some of this is various booby traps which were missed, but it seems plausible to me that not all of them are. (I'm trying to find sources from alchemists during the transition when Uranus came into focus; I'm hoping some of them would have noticed, and mentioned this change; unfortunately, the period of interest is a dark age for the history of occultism)

The discovery of electricity, the link between lightning and electric/biological phenomena, and the Chemical Revolution all have the hallmarks of Uranian influences; and the fact that quite a few explosives turn out to have weird other uses (who would think nitroglycerine would treat heart disease?) suggest that these are all linked to Uranus.

Modern chemistry then appears to be inherently Uranian; and this is something I'm looking further into.

Ceres is far and away the most interesting for my purposes: Romantic medicine and Romantic chemistry are both full of weird and unusual details, but things which contemporaries took for granted, and ascribed real force to. For example, there are plenty of scientific experiments in this period which demonstrated that "energy" moved from one substance to another when they mixed; and, to provide on example, even if the salt was removed, there were chemical properties which distinguished salt water from water which had never had salt dissolved into it. These are far, far more robust than anything we have today; and seem to have lasted for the period of Ceres' influence.

There's also Lamarckism; which had a great deal of evidence collected for it during the period Ceres was a planet; and not much before or after.

Vulcan seems to have related to the Luminiferous Aether; just like the Luminiferous Aether never fully materialized, neither did Vulcan. But, just like Vulcan kept appearing, all the way until at least 1909, there were experiments which appeared to confirm the existence of the Luminiferous Aether.

The timing for this was also intriguing, as the Luminiferous Aether acquired the form it would have until it vanished for good in 1915 in 1861 with Maxwell's equations; and this is surprisingly close to Vulcan's "discovery".

Pluto seems to have allowed for plenty of things which were impossible according to the prior understanding of things: heavier than air flight, GPS, space travel, computers, among other things. Many of these rely on relativity or quantum mechanics; and the gaping hole between the two is a Plutonian fracture running through modern physics. In terms of technology, it looks like chemical warfare in the modern sense is Plutonian; as is the modern electric grid.

Radio seems to be Plutonian, complete with all the applications thereof. Meanwhile, since 1900, The Decline Effect has set in, with everything from the energy released by nuclear fusion to the acidification of the oceans with C02 concentration in the atmosphere seemingly promising much larger effects, and then gradually whittling down as time passes.

The evidence from Ceres suggests that there may be a real change; but it looks like at the very least, the human understanding of reality changes drastically with new planets.
